01_day-night:
One dial, one whole day
DOMY Watch shows all 24 hours on a single face: noon stands at the top, midnight at the bottom, dusk on the right, dawn on the left. The small hand makes ONE turn per day, the large hand one per hour — and the lit part of the dial IS your daylight: it grows and shrinks with the seasons, exactly as your sky does.
02_pointer_prism:
The Prism pointer
Six diamonds split the day into six moods, like white light through a prism: yellow noon Joy, orange afternoon Zeal, red evening Passion, purple midnight Sorrow, blue pre-dawn Calm, green morning Renewal. The star's top tip always points at TRUE solar noon for your exact location — watch it lean east or west of 12.
47_palette_prism_paint:
Prism — Paint
Every pointer serves its colors in two worlds, and this is the first: PAINT mixes pigments the way painters always have — yellow, red and blue meeting in orange, purple and green. Noon wears the yellow of Joy, midnight the mourning purple.
48_palette_prism_light:
Prism — Light
The second world: LIGHT adds glowing beams the way screens and prisms do — red, green and blue summing toward white. Green takes the top here; that reversal IS the point of having two worlds. Switch them under Theme > Pointer.
49_palette_compass_paint:
Compass — Paint
Eight winds of the day in pigment: the noon arm, the two oranges of the working afternoon, sunset red, midnight purple, night blue, the cyan of dawn and the morning green — with the bottom arm giving up its diamond for the info slot.
50_palette_compass_light:
Compass — Light
The same eight winds projected as light. Dusk burns orange at 18h and dawn answers in azure at 6h — the two skies are exact complements, one looking back at the day, one looking forward.
51_palette_seasons:
Seasons
Four slim arms, four corners of the year: summer yellow at the top, autumn red on the right, winter blue at the bottom, spring green on the left. One palette serves both worlds — the year itself doesn't change with the color theory.
52_palette_trinity:
Trinity
Three arms at the ring's letters carry the three virtues: Faith in yellow at noon, Love in red at evening, Hope in blue before dawn. Each hue's third of the day centers on its arm's tip.
04_pointer_seasons:
The Seasons pointer
Four slim arms point at the four corners of the year: the top at the summer solstice, the bottom at the winter solstice, the sides at the equinoxes. Its palette is the year itself — summer yellow, autumn red, winter blue, spring green — and each arm's hover tells you the exact instant of its event and the meteorological season it centers.
05_pointer_compass:
The Compass pointer
Eight arms, eight winds of the day. The bottom arm gives up its diamond for an INFO SLOT you choose yourself: digital time, date, day length, your zodiac sign as art or text, or the Chinese year. The slot has its own switch under Elements and survives even when the pointer itself is hidden.
06_pointer_trinity:
The Trinity pointer
Three arms at the ring's letters carry the three virtues that remain: Faith in yellow at noon, Love in red at evening, Hope in blue before dawn. Each arm guards a third of the day and a pair of weekdays — and its hover tells the virtue's whole story.
07_umbra_full:
The Umbra — your night wheel
Under the colors turns a gray wheel: brightest at true solar noon, darkest at true midnight, mirrored left and right. In FULL contrast it sweeps the entire range from white to black in sixteen steps — a shadow clock inside the clock.
08_umbra_half:
Umbra: Half contrast
The same wheel, gentler: HALF contrast keeps to the middle grays, so the night wheel whispers instead of shouting. Pick the form too — Fine (16 shades), Coarse (13) or a perfectly smooth Gradient.
09_umbra_light:
Umbra: Light contrast
LIGHT contrast lives in the bright half of the scale — an airy, silver wheel for bright desktops and light wallpapers.
10_umbra_dark:
Umbra: Dark contrast
DARK contrast keeps to the deep half — a brooding, near-black wheel that lets the Aura's colors burn against it.
11_earth_moon:
Two travelers on the dial
The EARTH rides the year wheel once around per year — summer solstice at the top, winter at the bottom — switching between day and night art with your actual sky. The MOON rides its own cycle: new moon at the top, full moon at the bottom, always showing tonight's true phase. Around solstices, equinoxes and principal moon phases they GLOW.
12_earth_legend:
Hover the Earth
Point at the Earth and the legend opens: today's date, which day and week of the year it is, how deep you are into the season ("Summer 18th of 94 Days" — southern hemisphere and the tropics get their own truth), and your current zodiac sign with its exact dates.
13_moon_legend:
Hover the Moon
The Moon's legend gives the phase by name (with the exact instant when a principal phase is near), the illumination percentage, today's moonrise and moonset, and the day of the 29.53-day cycle. On dates when the moon skips a rise or a set — it happens once a month — the legend says so honestly.
14_diamond_legend:
Hover a diamond
Every Prism diamond spans exactly two zodiac signs of the year wheel. Its hover shows both signs with their dates — and full encyclopedic articles: the myth, the owner's art, the color and virtue the sign inherits from its arm, and the classical planet that rules it.
15_day_legend:
Hover the daylight
The colored arc is your day. Its legend measures it: how long the sun is up, the exact sunrise and sunset, and the extended day with twilight — from first light at dawn to the last at dusk.
16_night_legend:
Hover the night
The dark of the wheel answers with the night: its length, the sunset that starts it, the sunrise that ends it, and the true dark between dusk and dawn.
17_dawn_legend:
The morning twilight
The pale band before sunrise is the civil dawn — the sun is below the horizon but the sky already carries light. Its hover gives both boundary times: when dawn begins and when the sun itself appears.
18_dusk_legend:
The evening twilight
The matching band after sunset: the sun is gone but the light lingers until dusk. Hover it for the exact span — and watch both bands stretch dramatically if you move the clock toward the poles.
19_rings:
Rings, letters, metals
The ring's letters are real gold and silver art. DOMY spells its word with M, Y, Omega and D; MORPH answers with Greek. GOLD finish puts the triangle of three letters in gold, SILVER inverts every metal — and in Settings you can compose a CUSTOM ring: pick the Flame, Chalice or Seal layout and any letters from the library, then find your creation under Theme > Ring.
21_time_travel:
Time Travel
Right-click > Time Travel opens any moment at any place: your birthday in 1990, a solstice in Tokyo, a polar night in Tromso. The dial lives that moment for sixty seconds — sun, moon, seasons and all — then quietly returns to now.
22_ring_domy_gold:
DOMY in gold
The GOLD finish dresses the DOMY trio — M, Y and D, the triangle at 12, 20 and 4 — in gold and leaves Omega alone in silver. One glance at the metals tells you which family the ring belongs to.
23_ring_domy_silver:
DOMY in silver
The SILVER finish is the exact inverse: the triangle turns silver and Omega alone keeps the gold. Same letters, same places — only the metals trade sides.
24_weekday_planets:
Theme: Planets
Seven days, seven classical planets — Sunday's Sun, Monday's Moon, Tuesday's Mars, Wednesday's Mercury, Thursday's Jupiter, Friday's Venus, Saturday's Saturn. Every other theme in this chapter is a different mask over these same seven faces.
25_weekday_greek:
Theme: Greek gods
The same seven days wearing Greece: Helios, Selene, Ares, Hermes, Zeus, Aphrodite and Cronus. Hover any medallion for the god's full story — and for how his virtue and his vice color the arm he lives on.
26_weekday_norse:
Theme: Norse gods
The northern answer: Sol and Mani drive the sky's two lights, Tyr, Odin, Thor and Freya gave four weekdays their English names — and Loki stands on Saturday, the bound trickster as the north's own Cronus.
27_weekday_religion:
Theme: Religions
Seven living traditions, each on the day it holds sacred or fits best — from Christianity's Sunday to Judaism's Sabbath. A second set (Religions II) reaches older still: Egypt, Druidism, Zoroastrianism, Shamanism, Sikhism, Babylon and Voodoo.
28_weekday_professions:
Theme: Professions
The old week of callings: the Ruler, the Physician, the Soldier, the Merchant, the Priest, the Artist and the Farmer. Each coin is a trade the old world matched to its planet — hover one and its article tells you why.
29_slot_zodiac:
The slot as zodiac
The Info slot can wear your current zodiac sign as the owner's art — logo, constellation or the classic sign — and its hover opens the sign's full article with the exact date span.
30_slot_chinese:
The slot as Chinese year
Or let the Info slot speak Chinese: the current animal's medallion, the year's span in the hover — and the element paragraph that makes THIS return of the animal different from the last, Fire Horse against Metal Horse.
31_settings_location:
Your place on Earth
Search 45,000 cities as you type, or walk the combos continent by continent — the starred entries are each country's major cities. Latitude and longitude take manual fine-tuning down to your street, and every sun time on the dial follows.
32_settings_opacity:
Opacity
Three independent sliders: the Star (the pointer itself), the Aura's sunlight and the Aura's twilight. Each has its own Skin default button, so an experiment is never more than one click from home.
33_settings_sizes:
Element sizes
Every body on the dial scales to taste — Earth, Moon, the weekday medallions, the Compass slot and the ring letters — plus Hover enlarge, which grows whatever the cursor touches so small elements read without squinting.
34_settings_custom_ring:
Your own ring
Pick a layout — Flame (masculine, 4 letters), Chalice (feminine, 4) or Seal (union, all 6) — choose a library letter for every position, give it a unique name and Add. Your creation appears under Theme > Ring wearing the full gold and silver metal rules.
35_menu_elements:
The Elements switches
Everything on the dial has an off switch: Earth, Moon, the Weekday medallions, the Pointer, the Colorful arcs, the Seconds scale and the Compass slot. The right-click menu and the tray menu carry the same tree, and the Legend switch silences every hover at once. The Earth's own Clean-versus-Atmosphere choice has since moved to Theme ▸ Earth — this switch only turns the marker on or off.
36_ring_morph_silver:
MORPH in silver
The Greek ring in its silver finish: Pi, Omega and H silvered, M alone in gold. Flame and Chalice layouts always dress one trio in one metal and the lone opposite in the other; the Seal dresses all six letters in a single metal.
37_settings_palette:
The pointer palette
The active pointer's hues as big circles — hover one and it names the arm position it colors: Top, Bottom Left… the Compass answers in winds, North-East. Click to repaint it; your edit is remembered per pointer and style, and Reset returns the original.
38_settings_ring_tint:
The ring tint
Thirty-two circles recolor the whole clock body — a gold palette, twenty-one hues drawn from the designer's ring sheet, or any custom color. Whites and blacks never change, so the numerals stay crisp; the active swatch wears a white ring.
39_settings_language:
Language
Pick any of 68 languages: the first pick translates every text in the background — one-time internet — and caches it, so the language works offline ever after. Default returns to the shipped English originals.
40_settings_window:
The Settings window
One dialog holds everything personal: your place on Earth, the opacities, every element's size, the pointer palette, the ring tint, your own rings and the language. The next two pages walk it group by group.
53_settings_custom_hands:
Your own hands
Bring any three PNG images pointing UP — hours, minutes, seconds. Tell the dial where each one turns: Pivot X from the left (leave 'center' for symmetric hands) and Pivot Y in pixels from the bottom; pick the draw order and a unique name, and Add hands. Sizes are automatic — the seconds tip meets the ring, the minutes tip the minute arrows, the hours keep your model's own proportions — and colored art grays out so the clock tint can dress it. Your set appears under Design ▸ Hands.
41_tint_light_gold:
Light gold
The ring tint recolors the whole body of the clock — band, hands and the night wheel — while every white numeral and black tick stays exactly as drawn. This is the Naples gold of the owner's palette.
42_tint_dark_gold:
Dark gold
The deeper golds turn the dial into old brass. Because whites and blacks never tint, the scale stays as readable at midnight as the untouched gray original.
43_tint_green:
Deep pine
The green family — Deep Pine, Sage Steel, Smoke — sits the clock into forest wallpapers. Ring tint and pointer palette are independent: the Aura's colors burn unchanged over any body hue.
44_tint_blue:
Steel blue
The pipetted blues from the designer's ring sheet — Glaucous, Slate, Cadet — give the watch its nautical face. The Umbra follows the hue, so night deepens in the same key.
45_tint_lavender:
Lavender
The violet grays — Periwinkle, Lavender Gray — read soft on dark wallpapers. Thirty-two presets ship in Settings, and the Custom picker opens the whole spectrum.
46_tint_brown:
Brown
Espresso and Golden Brown land closest to a leather-and-wood instrument. One click in Settings > Ring tint changes the mood; Gray always brings back the original art.
54_menu_top_level:
The menu, opened
Right-click anywhere inside the dial's circle and the whole instrument opens beneath your cursor. Two groups organize everything that follows: Design decides how the clock LOOKS — pointer, palette, ring, hands, umbra, size — while Theme decides what its figures SHOW — Earth, weekday, Day slot, Info slot. Legend, Solar rotation and Click-through sit below both groups as simple toggles, then Settings, Time Travel, Encyclopedia and Guide open their own windows.
55_tray_menu:
The same tree, from the tray
Right-click the tray icon in the notification area and you meet the identical menu — same Design and Theme groups, same toggles, same dialogs. Wherever your cursor happens to be when the whim to change something strikes, the whole instrument is one right-click away. The tray is also the ONLY way back out of Click-through mode, since the dial itself stops taking clicks the moment that toggle is checked.
56_tick_anatomy:
Anatomy of the tick band
The ring's outer band carries four tiers of mark in one continuous ladder: twelve bold white ticks glow at the odd hours, eleven shorter gray ticks fill the even hours between them, and a single unique arrow shape claims the 12h/noon position — reserved for the ring letter that overlays it at render time. Between these hour marks, forty-eight medium ticks count the half-hours and two hundred eighty-eight thin ticks count every five minutes, for exactly three hundred sixty arrows in all: one every four minutes of your day.
57_tick_hover:
Hover an arrow
Every one of the 360 arrows is a door into three readings at once. Hovering opens the Day wheel's Time and Angle with the period word for that moment, the Year wheel's Date with its event or season and the day/week ordinals, and the Moon wheel's lunation ordinal, illumination percentage, phase name and cycle day — the whole instrument's memory, summoned from a single hover.
58_pointer_aurora:
The Aurora pointer
Aurora abandons arms and diamonds entirely: the day itself becomes a painted band, a dawn hue giving way to five hues that trace the real sunrise-to-sunset arc before a dusk hue closes the circle. It is the one pointer that always stays solar-rotated, regardless of the Solar rotation toggle — the sweep only means something pinned to true noon.
59_palette_aurora_paint:
Aurora — Paint
Aurora's own Paint world mixes seven pigment hues across its band: azure at dawn warming through green, yellow, orange and red toward a dark red dusk. Close in on the sweep and the transitions read as a single continuous gradient, not discrete diamonds — the palette IS the pointer here.
60_palette_aurora_light:
Aurora — Light
The same band in Aurora's Light world: azure opens into cyan, green, yellow and red before deepening to dark red at dusk. Set beside image 59's Paint sweep, the difference between mixing pigment and summing glow becomes obvious even without arms to anchor the comparison.
61_umbra_coarse:
Umbra: Coarse form
Where Fine steps through sixteen shades, Coarse simplifies the same night wheel into thirteen visibly chunkier bands — a form choice, independent of contrast. Shot here at Full contrast to match the existing Fine reference, only the step COUNT differs between the two.
62_umbra_gradient_form:
Umbra: Gradient form
Gradient abandons steps altogether: the night wheel sweeps from white to black with no visible bands at all, a seamless shadow clock. Form (Fine, Coarse, Gradient) and contrast (Full, Half, Light, Dark) combine independently — this is the smoothest of the three forms at the deepest of the four contrasts.
63_ring_domy_bronze:
DOMY in bronze
Bronze is the third finish, shipped since 2026-07-12 and shown here for the first time: the M/Y/D triangle takes on bronze's warm patina while Omega keeps its silver, the same accent rule Gold and Silver already follow with their own metal pair. One glance at the triangle's color still tells you the finish at a distance.
64_ring_numbers_seal:
NUMBERS, the third ring
Bundled alongside DOMY and MORPH, NUMBERS dresses a Seal layout in digits instead of letters — 12, 16, 20, Omega, 4 and 8 standing in for the usual six positions. All six wear the SAME metal at once, the rule every Seal layout follows regardless of which letters or numbers it carries — the two-triangle mixing rule belongs to Flame and Chalice alone.
65_menu_design_ring:
Design ▸ Ring, expanded
The Ring submenu lists every bundled face — DOMY, MORPH, NUMBERS — plus any custom ring you have composed in Settings, all above a separator; below it, the three letter finishes, Gold, Silver and Bronze, apply to whichever ring is active. Custom rings you build yourself join this same list, indistinguishable in the menu from the three the app ships with.
66_hands_classic:
Hands: CLASSIC
The CLASSIC pack draws all three hands as soft teardrops, a rounder silhouette than the current default. Centered here on the hub at a readable time, the hour, minute and — where shown — second hands each keep a distinct length, so their proportions read clearly even at a glance.
67_hands_steel:
Hands: STEEL
STEEL is the shipped default: straighter, more instrument-like blades than CLASSIC's teardrops. Framed at the same hub crop and the same time as image 66, the two packs sit side by side in spirit if not in pixels — pick whichever reads truer against your own wallpaper.
68_menu_design_hands:
Design ▸ Hands, expanded
The Hands submenu lists CLASSIC and STEEL, with the active pack checked — and grows a third entry the moment you add your own pack in Settings ▸ Custom hands. Like the Ring submenu, a custom hand pack sits in this list on equal footing with the two the app ships with.
69_menu_design_size:
Design ▸ Size, expanded
Five presets set the WHOLE window's diameter at once — 360, 540, 720, 1080 and 1440 pixels — distinct from the Settings ▸ Element sizes sliders, which resize individual bodies WITHIN a fixed window. The active preset, 720 by default, shows checked.
70_size_compare:
Small to large
The same dial at 360, 720 and 1440 pixels, captured at native pixel size so the comparison is honest — no image was resized afterward to fake the difference. Every element scales together: the ring, the pointer, the weekday medallions, all in the same proportion regardless of which of the five presets you choose.
71_menu_theme_earth:
Theme ▸ Earth, expanded
A Clean/Atmosphere radio pair sets the Earth marker's art style, and a Date checkbox beneath the separator adds a date label beside it — grayed out below the 540 px size preset, where there simply isn't room to letter it legibly.
72_earth_clean_vs_atmo:
Clean versus Atmosphere
Two Earth styles, captured at the same season and the same day-night phase so only the art itself differs: Clean renders the globe as flat, graphic continents; Atmosphere wraps it in a soft glowing haze. Both switch between day and night art with your actual sky regardless of which style is active.
73_weekday_planet_signs:
Theme: Planet signs
A sibling to the classical Planets theme, this one swaps each day's ruling body for its zodiacal glyph — the same seven-day logic, a different visual language for the same old astrological attributions.
74_weekday_egypt:
Theme: Egyptian gods
Seven days, seven of the Nile's own: Ra, Khonsu, Montu, Thoth, Amun, Hathor and Osiris stand in for the classical planets, each carrying the same weekday attribution dressed in Egyptian myth.
75_weekday_slavic:
Theme: Slavic gods
The northern Balkans and the Rus' answer in kind: Dažbog, Hors, Svetovid, Veles, Perun, Mokoš and Morana take the seven days, a pantheon far less globally familiar than Greek or Norse but no less complete in its seven-fold structure.
76_weekday_alchemy:
Theme: Alchemy
The old alchemists matched each day to a metal long before chemistry split them apart: Gold, Silver, Iron, Quicksilver, Tin, Copper and Lead, one still-life per weekday, echoing the same planet-metal correspondences that gave Sunday its gold and Monday its silver.
77_weekday_japan:
Theme: Japanese week
The yōbi carry their own five-element logic — Sun, Moon, Fire, Water, Wood, Metal, Earth — each medallion labeled with its kanji alongside the art, a seven-day week that arrived by an entirely different route than the Western one yet lands on the same seven days.
78_weekday_religion_alt:
Theme: Religions II
Where the first Religions theme reached Christianity, Judaism and their contemporaries, this second set reaches older or less commonly paired traditions: Mithraism, Druidism, Zoroastrianism, Shamanism, Sikhism, Babylon and Voodoo, one per weekday.
79_weekday_greek_colored:
Greek gods, in Colored
The same seven medallions as image 25, now in the freshly-shipped Colored finish — full-color art in place of a single bronze-plate metal. Framed identically to the existing Greek page, flip between the two to see how differently a full-color rendering reads against a monochrome one.
80_menu_theme_weekday_metal:
The metal-theme submenu
Greek gods, Norse gods and Professions are the three themes with real metal art behind them, so each opens its own nested Gold / Silver / Bronze / Colored radio group under its name — visible here for Greek gods. The other eight weekday themes have no metal variants at all and activate with a single click.
81_slot_ascendant:
The Ascendant badge
Where the zodiac badge answers 'what sign is the calendar in', Ascendant answers a faster question: which sign is rising on the eastern horizon RIGHT NOW — a value that changes roughly every two hours as the Earth turns beneath the zodiac belt. Shown here reading a different sign than the date alone would suggest, the point the screenshot exists to make.
82_menu_theme_slots_day:
Theme ▸ Day slot, expanded
Seven modes share this one position: Weekday (holding the full eleven-theme list plus a Names switch), Time, Date, Day length, Astrology, Ascendant and Chinese zodiac. The three text modes — Time, Date, Day length — gray out while the Pointer element itself is on, since there is nowhere left to draw them.
83_slot_info_time:
The Info slot, reading time
Switching to the Compass pointer frees its eight-armed bottom position into an Info slot of its own, defaulting here to a plain digital readout of the time. Compass, Trinity and Aurora all keep this slot permanently available; on Prism and Seasons it only appears once the Weekday or Pointer element is switched off.
84_slot_info_paired_weekday:
Two slots, two themes
With both the Day slot and the Info slot switched on at once, each can run its own independent weekday theme — Norse on one side, Greek on the other, both reading today's date through entirely different mythologies. Nothing forces the two slots to agree; that independence is the whole feature.
85_menu_theme_slots_info:
Theme ▸ Info slot, expanded
The Info slot's own submenu offers the identical seven modes as the Day slot's — Weekday, Time, Date, Day length, Astrology, Ascendant, Chinese zodiac — proof the two positions are peers, not a primary slot and a secondary afterthought.
86_colorful_on_off:
Colorful, on and off
The same daylight-to-twilight arc, captured twice at the same moment: Colorful ON tints it through its full hue range, Colorful OFF leaves plain white transparency in its place. A quieter, more minimal look is one menu click away without touching any other setting.
87_solar_rotation_off:
Solar rotation, switched off
With Solar rotation unchecked, the whole dial stands bolt upright — 12 and 24 exactly at top and bottom, no lean on the star, the Aura or the Umbra — useful for reading exact planet or season positions without the small daily tilt that true solar noon otherwise introduces. Every other screenshot in this Guide shows the toggle ON, its ordinary state.
88_tray_click_through:
Click-through, from the tray
Once Click-through is checked, the dial itself stops accepting any click at all — by design, since the whole point is a widget that never steals a click meant for the desktop beneath it. The tray menu, shown here with the toggle visibly checked, is the only way back out.
89_settings_theme_rotation:
Theme rotation
Check a handful of weekday themes, set an interval, and the dial cycles between them on its own — this screenshot ticks four or five to show the grid, plus a readable interval, per-theme metal dropdowns for the three metal-capable themes, and Follow ring color left unchecked so those dropdowns stay live rather than locked to the ring's own finish.
90_settings_system:
System
One checkbox closes the Settings dialog: Start with Windows, a standard-user registry entry that needs no elevation and no separate installer step — check it once and the dial is waiting at your next login.
